front.title: Outcome Binding: Approval over Predicted Effects, Executor Attestation of Observed Effects, and Machine-Detectable Divergence

abstract.t: Every signed-authorization format for agent actions proves approval of BYTES: the exact canonical action a human signed. What the action then did to the world is unproven. Permit formats stop at the moment before execution; outcome-attestation formats record what an executor claims happened afterward and explicitly leave the comparison between expected and actual to the relying party. This document specifies the comparison. A PREDICTED-EFFECTS object, signed inside the authorization payload, states the approved consequences as typed predicates with tolerance bounds (equality, upper and lower bounds, ranges, set equality, count ceilings, and required absence). An OBSERVED-EFFECTS attestation, signed by the executor after execution, states what happened. A deterministic evaluation compares the two and yields a closed result: in_bounds, divergent, or incomparable. A divergent or incomparable result is itself machine-readable, signed evidence that feeds action evidence graphs, where it forces the verdict to 'conflicted'. This BOUNDS the residual between approval and consequence; it does not close it. The observed effects are an executor claim: a lying executor defeats observation. Divergence becomes detectable and attributable, not impossible.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: An authorization receipt proves that a named human approved an exact action representation: canonical bytes, digested and signed . That proof is necessary and it is not sufficient, because the world does not execute bytes; it executes consequences. "Pay vendor 9" as approved bytes can become a payment of a different amount, to a different account, accompanied by a file deletion nobody approved. Today no artifact makes that gap machine-detectable: the approval names no consequences, the execution record names no approval bar, and the comparison between the two is everyone's job and therefore nobody's.
This document defines the three pieces of that comparison:

ol.li: The PREDICTED-EFFECTS object (): an array inside the receipt payload the human signs, stating the approved consequences as typed predicates with tolerance bounds. The human no longer approves only "these bytes"; the human approves "these bytes, whose effects will stay inside these bounds".
The OBSERVED-EFFECTS attestation (): a statement, signed by the executor AFTER execution, of the effects that actually occurred, bound to the receipt it executed under.

DIVERGENCE EVALUATION (): a deterministic, offline, replayable comparison of the two, with a closed result set. The result is signed evidence in its own right () and drives the evidence-graph verdict to 'conflicted' whenever effects diverged or could not be compared.

section.t: Honest scope, stated up front: this mechanism BOUNDS the residual between approval and consequence. It does not close it. The observed effects are a claim by the executor, and an executor that lies about what it did defeats observation entirely; what this document makes impossible is SILENT divergence by an executor whose attestations are later checked against reality, because its signed claim is then the contradiction. Predictions are only as good as the material fields the deployment chooses to predict, and tolerance bounds are policy: a bound wide enough to admit anything proves nothing. These limits are restated with their consequences in .

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

EFFECT: a discrete, observable consequence of executing an action, identified by an effect_type (the kind of consequence, e.g. "payment", "file_delete", "role_grant") and a target (the thing acted on). PREDICATE: a machine-evaluable constraint over the observed effects matching one (effect_type, target) pair. EXECUTOR: the party that carries out the approved action and signs the observed-effects attestation. RELYING PARTY: the party that evaluates divergence before relying on the action's evidence.

section.name: The Predicted-Effects Object
section.t: predicted_effects is a JSON array carried INSIDE the receipt payload the human signs, so the predictions are covered by the same signature, the same canonicalization, and the same one-time consumption as the action itself. Each entry names one constraint:
The predicate op set is CLOSED in this version:

dl.dd: the observed value is exactly the predicted string.
the observed value, as an exact decimal string, is at most / at least the predicted value.
min <= observed <= max, both bounds inclusive, min MUST NOT exceed max.
the observed values, as a set, equal the predicted set (order- and duplicate-insensitive). A grant of MORE scopes than predicted is divergence, not surplus.
the NUMBER of observed effects matching (effect_type, target) is at most the predicted value. Zero matches is a valid count of zero, not a missing observation.
NO observed effect may match (effect_type, target). This is how an approval states "and nothing of this destructive kind happens at all".

section.t: Comparison values are STRINGS, never JSON numbers. This is the same canonicalization-malleability rule monetary amounts follow throughout this receipt family: a JSON number can re-serialize into different bytes across implementations; a string is the byte sequence the human signed. An implementation encountering a numeric comparison value anywhere in a predicate or an observation MUST treat the evaluation as incomparable (), never as a pass. Ordered comparisons (lte, gte, range, count_lte) are performed as exact decimal-string comparisons: no floating point, so values beyond 2^53 and sub-cent fractions compare correctly and identically in every implementation.

Unknown predicate ops, and unknown members inside a prediction entry or a predicate object, MUST make the predicted_effects array malformed (evaluated as incomparable). The signer may have intended a constraint this evaluator would otherwise silently ignore; silently ignoring a signed constraint is a pass the human never granted.

The array is canonicalized with JCS and digested: predicted_effects_digest is the sha256 over the canonical bytes. Verifiers that transport the array separately from the payload MUST recompute this digest and refuse on mismatch.

section.name: The Observed-Effects Attestation
section.t: After execution, the executor signs an effect attestation bound to the receipt it executed under. This document extends the existing effect_attestation evidence node: today that node carries a single observed_effect_digest compared for exact equality against the approved committed effect digest. That path remains valid and unchanged; it is the degenerate case of this specification, a single eq predicate over one digest. The extension adds an observed_effects array:

Each observed entry carries effect_type and target (matched exactly against predictions), plus value (a string) for scalar predicates or values (an array of strings) for set_eq. Observed entries MAY carry additional members (timestamps, system-of-record references); only the compared fields are read. The asymmetry is deliberate: the SIGNED prediction is strict because it encodes intent, while the observation is a report that may carry operational metadata.

The attestation MUST be signed by the executor and verified against a key the relying party has pinned. As everywhere in this receipt family, VERIFIED (the signature holds over the canonical bytes) is never ACCEPTED (the executor key is trusted under a pinned root); they are separate checks and implementations MUST keep them separate. An attestation whose signature does not verify, or whose executor key is not pinned, is not weighed as effect evidence at all: at the evidence-graph layer it is inadmissible ('unverifiable'), which takes precedence over any divergence finding it might otherwise have produced.

The predictions the evaluation runs against are never taken from the presenter of the evidence. They are either read by the verifier out of the approved, signature-covered receipt payload, or pinned directly by the relying party's policy. A presenter cannot substitute looser predictions after the fact, because the predictions it would need to alter are inside the bytes the human signed.

section.name: Divergence Evaluation
section.t: Evaluation is a pure function of (predicted_effects, observed_effects) with a CLOSED result set. Same inputs give the same result and the same reasons in the same order, so any third party can replay the comparison offline.
Matching: for each prediction entry, the matching observations are those whose effect_type and target are exactly equal to the entry's. Per-op evaluation then proceeds:

ul.li: absent: zero matches is in_bounds; any match is divergent.
count_lte: the match count is compared to the bound; over the bound is divergent. A count is derived, so this op is always comparable.

All other ops require EXACTLY ONE matching observation. Zero matches is incomparable ("no observed effect for the predicate"). More than one is incomparable ("ambiguous"). A matching observation whose compared field is missing, non-string, or (for ordered ops) not an exact decimal string is incomparable.

section.t: Each result is one of:

dl.dd: at least one predicate evaluated and failed. The result carries a per-predicate reason of the form "predicted X, observed Y" for each failed predicate.

at least one predicate could not be evaluated: the observation is missing, ambiguous, or malformed, or the predicted_effects array itself is malformed. This is a REFUSAL with a reason. It MUST NOT be treated as a pass: an executor that omits or mangles the observation for an inconvenient predicate has not satisfied it.

section.t: Result precedence when predicates disagree: divergent over incomparable over in_bounds. A proven divergence is never masked by a neighboring predicate that merely could not be evaluated. Both non-pass classes drive the same downstream downgrade (), so this precedence never converts a refusal into a pass; it only chooses which finding leads the report.

Note on the term "divergence": published academic work uses "bounded divergence" for the property that a re-execution replays within bounds of a recorded run . The relation in this document is different: it compares an approved PREDICTION against an attested OBSERVATION of one execution, not one execution against its replay. The two are complementary and unrelated in mechanism.

section.name: The Divergence Result as Evidence
section.t: A divergence result is not a log line; it is evidence. When the effect attestation is presented as a node in an action evidence graph , the evaluation runs during policy replay and its result binds into the replayable verdict:

ul.li: divergent adds a machine-readable effect_divergence reason (carrying the per-predicate "predicted X, observed Y" details) and forces the graph verdict to 'conflicted'.
incomparable adds an effect_incomparable reason and likewise forces 'conflicted'. Missing or malformed observation is never a silent pass.

Both downgrades follow the existing verdict precedence: 'unverifiable' (for example, a bad executor signature) is strictly worse and is never softened to 'conflicted'; no finding ever moves a verdict toward 'admissible'.

The pre-existing exact-digest comparison keeps its effect_divergence and effect_commitment_missing semantics unchanged, and when an attestation carries both the digests and the arrays, both comparisons run: an in-bounds predicate result never masks a diverging exact digest.

section.t: Because the verdict is replayable, "the executor exceeded the approved bounds" becomes a claim any third party can recompute from the signed receipt, the signed attestation, and the policy, with no trust in the party that first noticed. Which action classes require an effect attestation at all, and within what deadline after execution, is declared per action in the action manifest ; an attestation that never arrives is missing evidence under the relying party's policy, not an in-bounds outcome.

section.name: Relationship to Prior Work
section.t: in-toto is the closest structural prior art: an expected layout signed in advance, link metadata attested by the parties that performed each step, and a verifier that compares the two. It is scoped to software supply chains, its comparisons are bit-exact artifact hashes and rule matches, and it has no tolerance semantics: there is no way to sign "the payment stays at or below 25000.00" and detect 26000.00 as a bounded violation. This document applies the expected-versus-attested shape to agent-action consequences, with typed predicates and tolerance bounds as the comparison language.

issues permits BEFORE execution over an exact digest of the intended operation. It carries no prediction of consequences and nothing post-execution to compare against; the permit's job ends at the property line this document starts from.

defines post-execution outcome-claim receipts, the other half of the pair. Its Section 8.3 states plainly that comparing an outcome claim against what was expected is "the responsibility of the relying party's verification logic, not the receipt format". That is a reasonable boundary for a receipt-format document to draw, and it is exactly the layer specified here: the signed prediction object, the deterministic comparison, and the closed result set that make every relying party's "verification logic" the same replayable computation. An outcome-claim receipt of that family could serve as the observed-effects carrier for this evaluation, provided its claims map to (effect_type, target, value) and its executor key is pinned by the relying party.

Checkout-mandate protocols in the payments space bind an exact cart, which is the eq case over one composite value; they do not express bounds, counts, or required absence. And as noted in , the "bounded divergence" of is a replay-determinism property, not this document's approved-prediction-versus-observed-effect relation.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: Executor honesty is a trust assumption, and this document states it plainly rather than engineering around it: the observed effects are whatever the executor signs. A lying executor defeats observation. What the mechanism changes is the executor's position afterward: its claim is signed, bound to the receipt, and comparable against systems of record, so a false attestation is durable, attributable evidence of the lie. Deployments that need more than accountability SHOULD obtain observations from a party other than the executor (the system of record itself, or an independent observer) and pin that party's key instead.

Predictions are only as good as the material fields. A prediction over fields that do not determine the action's real consequence binds nothing that matters; deployments MUST derive predicted_effects from the same material-field discipline the action's canonical representation uses. An absent predicate covers only the (effect_type, target) pattern it names: unpredicted effects outside every named pattern are NOT detected by this mechanism. A deployment that wants "and nothing else happened" must say so with absent predicates over the destructive effect types it fears, and accept that the enumeration is policy, not proof of a closed world.

Tolerance bounds are policy, and wide bounds prove little. An lte of one billion over a thousand-dollar payment is compliance theater with a signature on it. Relying parties SHOULD treat the tightness of the bounds as part of what they are relying on, not only the in_bounds result; the bounds are in the signed payload precisely so that they can be judged.

Fail-closed means a refusal with a reason. Malformed predictions, missing observations, ambiguous matches, and numeric values where strings are required all yield incomparable with a stated reason, and incomparable forces 'conflicted' downstream. None of these conditions may crash the evaluator or pass silently. Implementations MUST prove this against hostile input; the conformance suite for the reference implementation includes each such case as a reject vector.

The evaluation compares execution against approval; it does not revisit whether the approval was sufficient. An in_bounds result over a receipt that fails verification, binds a different action, or lacks required companion evidence rescues nothing: divergence evaluation composes with, and never substitutes for, the admissibility decision of the evidence-graph layer.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

front.title: in-toto: A framework to secure the integrity of software supply chains
Governing Dynamic Capabilities: Cryptographic Binding and Reproducibility Verification for AI Agent Tool Use
Permit Receipts for Permit-Before-Commit Authorization of AI-Agent and Workload External Effects
Execution Outcome Attestation for AI Agents and Automated Systems
Authorization Receipts for High-Risk Agent Actions
Action Evidence Graphs and Evidence Policy Replay for High-Risk Agent Actions (EP-AEG)
Agent Action Manifests: Declaring Control and Consequences for Agent-Reachable Actions

section.name: Implementation Status
section.t: A reference implementation is published Apache-2.0 in the EMILIA Protocol repository: the pure predicate evaluator with the closed op and result sets, exact decimal-string comparison, and the strict-prediction / tolerant-observation validation split (lib/evidence/effect-predicates.js), and the evidence-graph wiring in which divergent and incomparable results force the 'conflicted' verdict alongside the unchanged exact-digest path (lib/evidence/evidence-graph.js). A conformance suite (conformance/vectors/outcome-binding.v1.json) covers every predicate op passing and failing, numeric-instead-of-string rejection on both the prediction and observation sides, missing and ambiguous observations refusing as incomparable, an unpredicted destructive effect caught by an absent predicate, the divergent-over-incomparable precedence, the exact-digest backward-compatibility cases, and the relying-party pin overriding verifier-read predictions; a test harness runs every vector.
